content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 20, 1992

Kimberly Camp
Weintraub Genshlea & Sproul
P.O. Box 15208
Sacramento, CA 95881

Re: Your Request For Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-92-200

Dear Ms. Camp:

This is in reply to your letter requesting advice concerning
the campaign and lobbying provisions of the Political Reform
Act.1/ Because your question is a general inquiry, we are
treating it as a request for informal assistance pursuant to
Regulation 18329(c)(3).2/

The provisions of Proposition 73, added to the Political
Reform Act in June of 1988, which established statewide fiscal
year contribution limits has been invalidated. Service Employees
International Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices
Commission (9th Cir. Feb. 7, 1992) ___F.2d ___ [Dock. No. 89-
15771]. However, the court order did not invalidate the special
election and special runoff election contribution limits of
Proposition 73.

A person, including an individual, proprietorship, firm,
partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company
corporation or association, may contribute up to $1,000 per
candidate during a special election cycle and $1,000 per candidate
during a special runoff election cycle if a runoff
election is needed. "Special election cycle" means the day on

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Page 2
Kimberly Camp
I-92-200


which an elective office becomes vacant until the day of the
special election.  "Special runoff election cycle" means the day
after the special election until the day of the special runoff
election.  (Section 85305.)  In addition, if you are making
contributions to candidates for local office, you should contact
the local jurisdiction to determine if there are local
contribution limits in effect.

A lobbying firm must disclose on its Form 625 all monetary
and non-monetary contributions totaling $100 or more made by the
firm to elected state officers, state candidates or committees
supporting such officers or candidates.  (Section 86114.)

If I can be of further assistance, please contact me at
(916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Colleen McGee
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division

sections.question_synthetic: The requestor asked a general inquiry regarding campaign and lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act, specifically concerning contribution limits following the invalidation of Proposition 73's statewide fiscal year contribution limits.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC clarified that while Proposition 73's statewide fiscal year contribution limits were invalidated, the special election and special runoff election contribution limits of $1,000 per candidate remain valid. Additionally, lobbying firms must disclose contributions of $100 or more to elected state officers and candidates on Form 625.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document is marked as informal assistance request; no formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections are present in standard format, but the legal advice and response are clearly identifiable. Footer information is partially cut off due to OCR quality.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The requestor asked a general inquiry regarding campaign and lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act, specifically concerning contribution limits following the invalidation of Proposition 73's statewide fiscal year contribution limits.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC clarified that while Proposition 73's statewide fiscal year contribution limits were invalidated, the special election and special runoff election contribution limits of $1,000 per candidate remain valid. Additionally, lobbying firms must disclose contributions of $100 or more to elected state officers and candidates on Form 625.

embedding.summary: FPPC informal advice letter addressing campaign contribution limits under the Political Reform Act, explaining that special election and special runoff election contribution limits ($1,000 per candidate) remain valid despite invalidation of Proposition 73's statewide fiscal year limits, and clarifying lobbying firm disclosure requirements.
